---
title: "Explore model collinearity"
author: "LC"
date: "2026-01-23"
output: html_document
---

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(ggplot2)
library(reshape2)
library(proxy)

bd <- "/data00/leonardo/RSA/analyses/RATINGS"

subs_file <- "/data00/leonardo/RSA/sub_list.txt"
subs <- sprintf("%02d",readLines(subs_file) %>% as.numeric)

# Load the preprocessed emotion ratings file
# which was prepared with the 02_prepare_ratings_EMOTION.Rmd
# located in /data00/leonardo/RSA/prep_scripts/05_prepare_ratings
df_emotion <- read_csv(paste0(bd,"/","emotion_ratings.csv")) %>% select(sub, starts_with("r_"))
df_arousal <- read_csv(paste0(bd,"/","arousal_ratings.csv")) %>% select(sub, r_arousal)
df_aroval <- read_csv(paste0(bd,"/","aroval_ratings.csv")) %>% select(sub, starts_with("r_"))

```


# Edoardo code for calculating distances

```{r}

# NB: this code has Edoardo modifications for mahalanobis distance

# ----------- DDOS - Do Distance Or Similarity -----------
# Computes a representational dissimilarity matrix (RDM) between observations.
#
# INPUT:
# - X: observations-by-variables matrix
#     rows    = stimuli / conditions
#     columns = features (e.g., voxels, ratings)
#
# Distances:
# - dist() operates on rows (used directly)
# - cor() operates on columns, so X is transposed internally
# - Mahalanobis distance computed as: sqrt((x_i − x_j)^T Σ^−1 (x_i − x_j)),
#   where Σ is the feature covariance matrix estimated from X. (cf. Walther et al 2016)
#
# Supported methods:
# - euclidean, cosine, pearson, spearman, mahalanobis
#
# OUTPUT:
# - Lower-triangular vector of the observation-by-observation
#   dissimilarity matrix (length = (n^2 − n)/2)
#
# Example:
# rdm_vec <- DDOS_vec(X, method = "cosine")

DDOS_vec <- function(X, method, normalization = FALSE) {
  
  # Step 0) input hygiene checks
  # # If X is a plain vector, treat it as a single-column matrix (n_observations x 1)
  if (is.vector(X)) {
    X <- matrix(X, ncol = 1)
  }
  # # Make sure X is numeric (fail early if not)
  X <- as.matrix(X)
  # # Store dimensions (readability)
  n_obs  <- nrow(X)   # number of observations / stimuli (rows)
  n_feat <- ncol(X)   # number of features / variables (cols)
  
  # Step 1.1) Special case: single-column input
  # If there is only 1 feature, we can only compute distances between rows using that one variable.
  if (n_feat == 1) {
    # Need at least 2 observations to define any distance
    if (n_obs < 2) {
      warning("Too few rows to compute RDM for single-column input.")
      return(NA_real_)
    }
    
    # # Extract the single variable as a numeric vector
    x <- as.numeric(X[, 1])
    
    # # If method is pearson/spearman, treat it as a rank/correlation concept:
    # with 1D data, correlation-based dissimilarity is not meaningful as a matrix
    # between rows, so we approximate with Euclidean distance:
    # - Spearman: Euclidean on ranks
    # - Pearson : Euclidean on raw values
    if (method %in% c("pearson", "spearman")) {
      x_use <- if (method == "spearman") rank(x) else x          # rank-transform only for Spearman
      D     <- stats::dist(x_use, method = "euclidean")          # compute 1D pairwise Euclidean distances
      return(as.vector(D))                                       # dist() already returns the lower triangle vector
    }
    
    # # Otherwise: let dist() compute the requested metric (if supported)
    D <- stats::dist(X, method = method)                         # for single-column, dist uses rows as items
    return(as.vector(D))                                         # return lower triangle vector
  }
  
  # Step 1.2) General case: matrix with >=2 rows and >=2 columns
  # # If X is too small, distances/correlations are not meaningful
  if (n_obs < 2 || n_feat < 2) {
    warning("Matrix too small to compute full RDM. Returning NA.")
    return(NA_real_)
  }
  
  # # Optional standardisation:
  # - always center (subtract column means)
  # - optionally scale (divide by column sd) if normalization == TRUE
  X <- scale(X, center = TRUE, scale = normalization)
  
  # Step 2) Compute distance matrix (n_obs x n_obs)
  if (method == "mahalanobis") {
    
    # # Estimate covariance of the feature space across observations (columns = variables)
    S <- stats::cov(X, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
    # Invert covariance matrix (needed for Mahalanobis). If S is singular/non-invertible, we return NA
    S_inv <- tryCatch(
      solve(S),
      error = function(e) {
        warning("Covariance matrix not invertible; returning NA.")
        return(NA)
      }
    )
    # If inversion failed, exit
    if (is.na(S_inv)[1]) return(NA_real_)
    # Allocate the full distance matrix
    D <- matrix(0, n_obs, n_obs)
    # Compute pairwise Mahalanobis distance for each pair of observations (i, j)
    for (i in 1:(n_obs - 1)) {
      for (j in (i + 1):n_obs) {
        # Difference vector between the two observations (1 x n_feat)
        d <- X[i, , drop = FALSE] - X[j, , drop = FALSE]
        # Mahalanobis distance: sqrt( d %*% S_inv %*% t(d) )
        D[i, j] <- sqrt(d %*% S_inv %*% t(d))                    # scalar result
        D[j, i] <- D[i, j]                                       # symmetry
      }
    }
    
  } else {
    
    # # Other methods:
    # - pearson/spearman: 1 - correlation between observations (rows)
    # - euclidean: dist() on rows
    # - cosine: 1 - cosine similarity (using simil() function)
    D <- switch(
      method,
      pearson  = 1 - stats::cor(t(X), method = "pearson",  use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
      spearman = 1 - stats::cor(t(X), method = "spearman", use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
      euclidean = as.matrix(stats::dist(X, method = "euclidean")),
      cosine    = 1 - as.matrix(simil(X, method = "cosine")),
      stop("Unsupported method: ", method)
    )
  }
  
  # # Step 3) Return lower triangle as a vector (RDM vector form)
  # Keep only strictly-lower-triangular entries and drop NA entries
  return(as.vector(D[lower.tri(D) & !is.na(D)]))
}
```


# Edoardo code fixed so that we can calculate also mahalanobis for values
(It's the same as euclidean)
```{r}
# ----------- DDOS - Do Distance Or Similarity -----------
# Computes a representational dissimilarity matrix (RDM) between observations.
#
# INPUT:
# - X: observations-by-variables matrix
#     rows    = stimuli / conditions
#     columns = features (e.g., voxels, ratings)
#
# Distances:
# - dist() operates on rows (used directly)
# - cor() operates on columns, so X is transposed internally
# - Mahalanobis distance computed as: sqrt((x_i − x_j)^T Σ^−1 (x_i − x_j)),
#   where Σ is the feature covariance matrix estimated from X. (cf. Walther et al 2016)
#
# Supported methods:
# - euclidean, cosine, pearson, spearman, mahalanobis
#
# OUTPUT:
# - Lower-triangular vector of the observation-by-observation
#   dissimilarity matrix (length = (n^2 − n)/2)
#
# Example:
# rdm_vec <- DDOS_vec(X, method = "cosine")

DDOS_vec <- function(X, method, normalization = FALSE) {
  
  # Step 0) Input hygiene checks
  if (is.vector(X)) {
    X <- matrix(X, ncol = 1)
  }
  X <- as.matrix(X)
  n_obs  <- nrow(X)
  n_feat <- ncol(X)
  
  # Step 1.1) Single-column input
  if (n_feat == 1) {
    if (n_obs < 2) {
      warning("Too few rows to compute RDM for single-column input.")
      return(NA_real_)
    }
    x <- as.numeric(X[, 1])
    
    # Correlation methods approximate with Euclidean
    if (method %in% c("pearson", "spearman")) {
      x_use <- if (method == "spearman") rank(x) else x
      D <- stats::dist(x_use, method = "euclidean")
      return(as.vector(D))
    }
    
    # Mahalanobis for 1D = Euclidean / optionally standardized
    if (method == "mahalanobis") {
      D <- stats::dist(x, method = "euclidean")
      D <- as.vector(D) / sd(x)  # optional standardization by variance
      return(D)
    }
    
    # Other methods use dist() directly
    D <- stats::dist(X, method = method)
    return(as.vector(D))
  }
  
  # Step 1.2) General case: >=2 rows and >=2 columns
  if (n_obs < 2 || n_feat < 2) {
    warning("Matrix too small to compute full RDM. Returning NA.")
    return(NA_real_)
  }
  
  # Optional standardization
  X <- scale(X, center = TRUE, scale = normalization)
  
  # Step 2) Compute distance matrix
  if (method == "mahalanobis") {
    S <- stats::cov(X, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
    S_inv <- tryCatch(
      solve(S),
      error = function(e) {
        warning("Covariance matrix not invertible; returning NA.")
        return(NA)
      }
    )
    if (is.na(S_inv)[1]) return(NA_real_)
    
    D <- matrix(0, n_obs, n_obs)
    for (i in 1:(n_obs - 1)) {
      for (j in (i + 1):n_obs) {
        d <- X[i, , drop = FALSE] - X[j, , drop = FALSE]
        D[i, j] <- sqrt(d %*% S_inv %*% t(d))
        D[j, i] <- D[i, j]
      }
    }
    
  } else {
    # Other methods
    D <- switch(
      method,
      pearson  = 1 - stats::cor(t(X), method = "pearson",  use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
      spearman = 1 - stats::cor(t(X), method = "spearman", use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
      euclidean = as.matrix(stats::dist(X, method = "euclidean")),
      cosine    = 1 - as.matrix(simil(X, method = "cosine")),
      stop("Unsupported method: ", method)
    )
  }
  
  # Step 3) Return lower-triangular vector
  return(as.vector(D[lower.tri(D) & !is.na(D)]))
}

```




```{r}

# method = "euclidean"
method = "mahalanobis"

# Example for emotion data
X_emotion <- df_emotion %>% select(starts_with("r_"))
rdm_emotion <- DDOS_vec(X_emotion, method = method)

# For arousal (single column r_arousal)
X_arousal <- df_arousal %>% select(starts_with("r_"))
rdm_arousal <- DDOS_vec(X_arousal, method = method)

# For arousal + valence
X_aroval <- df_aroval %>% select(starts_with("r_"))
rdm_aroval <- DDOS_vec(X_aroval, method = method)

```




```{r}
# Step 1: Combine RDM vectors into a single data frame
rdm_df <- data.frame(
  emotion = rdm_emotion,
  arousal = rdm_arousal,
  aroval  = rdm_aroval
)

# Step 2: Compute correlation matrix
corr_mat <- cor(rdm_df, method = "pearson")  # you can use "spearman" if you prefer

# Step 3: Convert to long format for ggplot
corr_long <- as.data.frame(as.table(corr_mat))
names(corr_long) <- c("Var1", "Var2", "Correlation")

# Step 4: Plot using ggplot2
ggplot(corr_long, aes(x = Var1, y = Var2, fill = Correlation)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Correlation, 2)), size = 5) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red", midpoint = 0) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Correlation Matrix of RDMs",
       x = "", y = "") +
  coord_fixed()

```
